mn47:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn47:10.1: And if, *bhikkhus*, others were to ask that *bhikkhu* thus:
mn47:10.2: ‘But what are the indications, what are the inferences, by which you say this—
mn47:10.3: “This venerable one has refrained not out of fear; this venerable one is not one who has refrained because of fear.
mn47:10.4: Because he is free of passion, he does not pursue sensual pleasures, because of the destruction of passion”?’
mn47:10.5: Answering rightly, *bhikkhus*, that *bhikkhu* should answer thus:
mn47:10.6: ‘For whether this venerable one is dwelling in the *saṅgha* or dwelling alone, whether those there are doing well or doing badly, whether they are instructing a group there, whether some are seen in relation to material gains, or whether some are not stained by material gain, this venerable one does not look down on them because of that.
mn47:10.7: And this I have heard in the presence of the *Bhagavā*, received in his presence—
mn47:10.8: “I have refrained not out of fear; I am not one who has refrained because of fear. Because I am free of passion, I do not pursue sensual pleasures, because of the destruction of passion.”
mn47:11.1: There, *bhikkhus*, the Tathāgata himself should be questioned further:
mn47:11.2: ‘Are there in the Tathāgata any defiled things knowable by eye and ear, or not?’
mn47:11.3: Answering, *bhikkhus*, the Tathāgata would answer thus:
mn47:11.4: ‘There are no defiled things knowable by eye and ear in the Tathāgata.’
mn47:12.1: ‘Are there in the Tathāgata any mixed things knowable by eye and ear, or not?’
mn47:12.2: Answering, *bhikkhus*, the Tathāgata would answer thus:
mn47:12.3: ‘There are no mixed things knowable by eye and ear in the Tathāgata.’
mn47:13.1: ‘Are there in the Tathāgata any pure things knowable by eye and ear, or not?’
mn47:13.2: Answering, *bhikkhus*, the Tathāgata would answer thus:
mn47:13.3: ‘There are pure things knowable by eye and ear in the Tathāgata.
mn47:13.4: That is my path, that is my range, yet I am not made of that.’
mn47:14.1: A teacher who speaks thus, *bhikkhus*, is one whom a disciple may rightly approach for hearing the *dhamma*.
mn47:14.2: That teacher teaches him the *dhamma*, progressively higher and more sublime, with its counterparts of dark and bright.
mn47:14.3: As, *bhikkhus*, the teacher teaches the *dhamma* to the *bhikkhu* progressively higher and more sublime, with its counterparts of dark and bright, so, by directly knowing that *dhamma*, he comes to certainty regarding a certain *dhamma* among the *dhammas*, and he becomes confident in the teacher:
mn47:14.4: ‘The *Bhagavā* is perfectly awakened, the *dhamma* is well proclaimed by the *Bhagavā*, the *saṅgha* has practiced well.’
mn47:15.1: And if, *bhikkhus*, others were to ask that *bhikkhu* thus:
mn47:15.2: ‘But what are the indications, what are the inferences, by which you say this—
mn47:15.3: “The *Bhagavā* is perfectly awakened, the *dhamma* is well proclaimed by the *Bhagavā*, the *saṅgha* has practiced well”?’
mn47:15.4: Answering rightly, *bhikkhus*, that *bhikkhu* should answer thus:
mn47:15.5: ‘Here, friends, I approached the *Bhagavā* for hearing the *dhamma*.
mn47:15.6: The *Bhagavā* taught me the *dhamma*, progressively higher and more sublime, with its counterparts of dark and bright.
mn47:15.7: As the *Bhagavā* taught me the *dhamma*, progressively higher and more sublime, with its counterparts of dark and bright, so, by directly knowing that *dhamma*, I came to certainty regarding a certain *dhamma* among the *dhammas*, and I became confident in the teacher—
mn47:15.8: “The *Bhagavā* is perfectly awakened, the *dhamma* is well proclaimed by the *Bhagavā*, the *saṅgha* has practiced well.”
mn47:16.1: For anyone at all, *bhikkhus*, whose confidence in the Tathāgata has become settled, rooted, and established by these indications, these words, these expressions, this is called confidence with grounds, rooted in seeing.
mn47:16.2: It is firm, not to be shaken by ascetic or brahmin or deva or Māra or Brahmā or anyone at all in the world.
mn47:16.3: This, *bhikkhus*, is how investigation of the Tathāgata by way of *dhamma* takes place.
mn47:16.4: And in this way the Tathāgata is well established in the principles of *dhamma*.”
mn47:16.6: Those *bhikkhus*, pleased, rejoiced in what the *Bhagavā* had said.
mn47:16.7: The seventh, The Inquirer, is finished.
mn47:2.1: “An investigating *bhikkhu*, *bhikkhus*, who does not know another’s state of mind, should make an investigation of the Tathāgata so as to know: ‘Is he perfectly awakened or not?’”
mn47:3.1: “Our *dhammas*, *bhante*, are rooted in the *Bhagavā*, guided by the *Bhagavā*, with the *Bhagavā* as refuge. It would be good, *bhante*, if the meaning of this statement would become clear from the *Bhagavā* himself. Having heard it from the *Bhagavā*, the *bhikkhus* will remember it.”
mn47:3.2: “Then listen, *bhikkhus*, and apply your mind well; I will speak.”
mn47:4.1: “An investigating *bhikkhu*, *bhikkhus*, who does not know another’s state of mind should examine the Tathāgata in regard to two *dhammas*, *dhammas* knowable by eye and ear:
mn47:4.2: ‘Are there in the Tathāgata any defiled things knowable by eye and ear, or not?’
mn47:4.3: While examining him, he understands thus:
mn47:4.4: ‘There are no defiled things knowable by eye and ear in the Tathāgata.’
mn47:5.1: When, while examining him, he understands thus:
mn47:5.2: ‘There are no defiled things knowable by eye and ear in the Tathāgata,’ then he examines him further:
mn47:5.3: ‘Are there in the Tathāgata any mixed things knowable by eye and ear, or not?’
mn47:5.4: While examining him, he understands thus:
mn47:5.5: ‘There are no mixed things knowable by eye and ear in the Tathāgata.’
mn47:6.1: When, while examining him, he understands thus:
mn47:6.2: ‘There are no mixed things knowable by eye and ear in the Tathāgata,’ then he examines him further:
mn47:6.3: ‘Are there in the Tathāgata any pure things knowable by eye and ear, or not?’
mn47:6.4: While examining him, he understands thus:
mn47:6.5: ‘There are pure things knowable by eye and ear in the Tathāgata.’
mn47:7.1: When, while examining him, he understands thus:
mn47:7.2: ‘There are pure things knowable by eye and ear in the Tathāgata,’ then he examines him further:
mn47:7.3: ‘Has this venerable one long entered upon this wholesome *dhamma*, or has he entered upon it only recently?’
mn47:7.4: While examining him, he understands thus:
mn47:7.5: ‘This venerable one has long entered upon this wholesome *dhamma*; this venerable one has not entered upon it only recently.’
mn47:8.1: When, while examining him, he understands thus:
mn47:8.2: ‘This venerable one has long entered upon this wholesome *dhamma*; this venerable one has not entered upon it only recently,’ then he examines him further:
mn47:8.3: ‘This venerable *bhikkhu* is well known and has attained fame; do certain drawbacks appear in him here?’
mn47:8.4: As long as a *bhikkhu* is not well known and has not attained fame, certain drawbacks do not yet appear in him here, *bhikkhus*.
mn47:8.5: But when, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* is well known and has attained fame, then certain drawbacks do appear in him here.
mn47:8.6: While examining him, he understands thus:
mn47:8.7: ‘This venerable *bhikkhu* is well known and has attained fame, but certain drawbacks do not appear in him here.’
mn47:9.1: When, while examining him, he understands thus:
mn47:9.2: ‘This venerable *bhikkhu* is well known and has attained fame, but certain drawbacks do not appear in him here,’ then he examines him further:
mn47:9.3: ‘This venerable one has refrained not out of fear; this venerable one is not one who has refrained because of fear.
mn47:9.4: Because he is free of passion, he does not pursue sensual pleasures, because of the destruction of passion.’
mn47:9.5: While examining him, he understands thus:
mn47:9.6: ‘This venerable one has refrained not out of fear; this venerable one is not one who has refrained because of fear.
mn47:9.7: Because he is free of passion, he does not pursue sensual pleasures, because of the destruction of passion.’
